original_hypothesis.title: Stale bridgedToCanonical entry due to wrong deletion in ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken can re-enable old bridged token in future code paths and break canonical↔bridged bijection
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken attempts to remove canonical mapping when migrating, but it deletes bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] (a no-op by construction) instead of deleting bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]. This leaves stale canonical metadata on the old bridged token forever. While btokenOld_ is blacklisted for sendToken, other code paths (future upgrades, off-chain systems, or direct uses of bridgedToCanonical) may treat the old token as canonical-backed, enabling confusion or potential mint/burn routing errors.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect (wrong key deletion) flagged by Phase A invariants. Even if not immediately exploitable today due to blacklist checks in sendToken, it is a high-value correctness issue that can become exploitable after upgrades or via any missing blacklist check elsewhere.

proof_of_signal.lines: changeBridgedToken(): delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]; btokenOld_ never cleared
proof_of_signal.pattern: incorrect mapping deletion leaving stale bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

preconditions: Owner executes changeBridgedToken for a canonical token that already has an old bridged token deployed
System later relies on bridgedToCanonical(oldToken) without checking btokenBlacklist[oldToken]

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: stale state due to wrong delete key
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bijection invariant breaks; old token continues to look canonical-linked

analysis_questions: Are there any current code paths that use bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist?
Could _handleMessage be reached with old btoken via any route other than sendToken (e.g., bridge callback data crafting if other bug exists)?
Do off-chain systems (indexers/relayers) rely on bridgedToCanonical for security decisions?
validation_requirements: Search for all reads of bridgedToCanonical and confirm consistent blacklist gating
Add regression test that old btoken mapping is cleared after migration

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After a migration, an upgraded function (or external integration) checks bridgedToCanonical[token].addr != 0 to accept a bridged token, but forgets to check btokenBlacklist. Attacker uses old token to trigger unintended burns/mints or bypass token mapping restrictions.
prerequisites: Subsequent code path/integration that uses bridgedToCanonical without blacklist check

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential incorrect mint/burn routing or acceptance of deprecated bridged token; could lead to loss if it enables bridging with a deprecated asset representation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users holding deprecated bridged tokens; potentially all users if routing breaks
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fix via upgrade + state cleanup/migration script

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing state invariant tests for canonical↔bridged bijection post-migration

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor existence of bridgedToCanonical for blacklisted btokens

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
